nativeRange.habitat: Coastal marine, estuarine, and tidal river mouths along the Pacific coast; adults are principally marine, juveniles use brackish and occasionally freshwater estuaries as nursery habitat

decorPreferences: open swimming space — avoid excessive rockwork
robust hardscape (driftwood, large smooth rocks) — plants not suitable as adults require brackish/marine
strong filtration — large messy predator with high bioload

diet.notes: The Colombian Shark Catfish is a carnivore that feeds on fish and invertebrates in the wild. In captivity it accepts sinking carnivore pellets and large frozen foods such as krill, shrimp, lance fish, whitebait, and mussels; earthworms are also taken readily. Juveniles will accept frozen bloodworm and brine shrimp, but adults should be transitioned to larger, chunkier items as they grow. These fish feed predominantly at night, so target-feeding is advisable when they share a tank with faster-moving species. Feeder goldfish are best avoided — they are nutritionally poor and carry a significant disease risk.

goodWith: large euryhaline fish of similar size (monos, scats, archerfish, large target perch)
figure-eight puffers (as adults in brackish — with caution)
avoidWith: small fish that will be eaten (anything under ~8–10 cm)
small invertebrates (shrimp, snails — will be consumed)
freshwater-only species unable to tolerate brackish progression

Wikimedia Commons image showing juvenile Colombian Shark Catfish, the life stage typically seen in trade.

summary: The Colombian Shark Catfish is sold as a freshwater juvenile but requires a full transition to brackish and then near-marine conditions as it matures. Adults reach 35 cm, carry venomous dorsal and pectoral spines, and are paternal mouthbrooders. Keeping one correctly is a specialist commitment.
careNotes: The Colombian Shark Catfish (Ariopsis seemanni) is one of the most consistently mis-sold fish in the freshwater trade. Juveniles appear in shops at 5–8 cm labelled as freshwater catfish, capitalising on their shark-like silhouette and silvery sheen. In reality, this species is a coastal marine ariid catfish; wild adults live in the sea, and juveniles use estuaries only as temporary nursery habitat. Long-term survival in pure freshwater is not possible — fish kept in fresh water deteriorate over months, developing osmotic stress, immune compromise, and shortened lifespans. The correct approach is to plan this progression from the outset. Juveniles can be maintained short-term at near-freshwater specific gravity (around SG 1.000–1.002), but subadults need to be moved into true brackish water (SG 1.005–1.010), and adults require near-marine or marine salinity (SG 1.015–1.025). Any increase in specific gravity must be gradual — no more than 0.001–0.002 per day — to avoid osmotic shock. There is no option to simply 'upgrade later'; the salt requirement increases with growth, and rehoming a fish that has reached 30 cm or more is extremely difficult. Tank size must be substantial: a minimum 400 L (180 cm footprint) is recommended for a small group of three, with powerful filtration as these are large, messy, high-bioload carnivores. They are active mid-water and surface swimmers and must have a tightly secured lid — escape risk is high. The venomous dorsal and pectoral spines are capable of delivering a painful sting; handle with extreme caution using thick gloves or a container, never bare hands. Colombian Shark Catfish are shoaling fish and should be kept in groups of three or more; solitary individuals are prone to stress. Tankmates must be large, robust, and euryhaline — suitable partners include monodactylids (monos), scatophagids (scats), archerfish, and other large brackish specialists. Any freshwater-only species cannot share their tank long-term, as water parameters must trend toward marine. These fish are copper-sensitive like most catfishes — never use copper-based medications. Water quality must be pristine; high nitrates accelerate the health decline seen in inappropriately fresh water.
breedingNotes: Ariopsis seemanni is a paternal mouthbrooder with one of the most remarkable reproductive strategies in the family Ariidae. Males incubate a small clutch of very large, yolk-rich eggs — often described as golf-ball sized — entirely within their oral cavity for approximately two months. During brooding the male cannot feed. Fertilised eggs and even early-stage fry are brooded in the mouth until the young are advanced and self-sufficient. Captive breeding has not been reliably reported in home aquaria; the species almost certainly requires near-marine or marine salinity and very large tanks to spawn naturally. The species' mouthbrooding strategy produces a small number of large, well-developed offspring rather than the mass spawning typical of open-water broadcast spawners.

fish.breedingNotes: The Colombian Shark Catfish is a paternal mouthbrooder. Males carry a small clutch of very large, yolk-rich eggs — often described as golf-ball sized — entirely within their oral cavity for approximately two months, during which they do not feed. Breeding has not been reliably achieved in home aquaria, and success would most likely require near-marine or marine salinity, very large tanks, and undisturbed brooding conditions.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Frequently mis-sold as a freshwater community fish. This species is a coastal marine ariid catfish; juveniles tolerate low salinity only temporarily, and long-term keeping requires a full progression from brackish (SG 1.005–1.010) to near-marine or marine conditions. Adults reach 35 cm and cannot be rehomed easily. Long-term success demands a conscientious commitment to euryhaline husbandry.
